title: Measure cytokine and anti-TREM2 effects on myeloid, NK and T cell activity
text:
The primary objective of this research task is to elucidate the effects of cytokines in conjunction with the E3C7 monoclonal antibody (mAb) on the activation and functional modulation of myeloid, natural killer (NK), and T cells. This will be achieved through a series of in-vitro assays designed to measure immune cell activity, specifically focusing on the proliferative potential and cytokine secretion of these cells. The experimental design involves co-culturing human monocyte-derived macrophages (huMDMs), NK cells, and T cells, both in the presence and absence of the anti-TREM2 mAb and cytokines, to serve as controls. The assays will employ flow cytometry to quantify cell proliferation and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) alongside quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) to assess cytokine secretion levels.

 Data collection will be meticulously conducted using these advanced methodologies to ensure high precision and reproducibility. Flow cytometry will provide quantitative data on cell proliferation by measuring the division rate of NK and T cells, while ELISA and qPCR will offer insights into the cytokine profiles secreted by these immune cells under different experimental conditions. This dual approach allows for a comprehensive analysis of both cellular and molecular responses, facilitating a robust understanding of the immune-modulating effects of the cytokine and anti-TREM2 mAb combination.

 The results will be analyzed using a data-driven framework, incorporating statistical methods to compare the proliferative and secretory responses between treated and control groups. Machine learning analytics may be applied to identify patterns and correlations within the data, enhancing the interpretation of the cytokine and mAb effects on immune cell activity. This analysis will contribute to the broader objective of identifying optimal cytokine combinations for therapeutic applications, as outlined in the overarching research aims (EIC Transition 2022-2024, Objective 1) [EIC AIMS, p.1]. The findings from this task will inform subsequent stages of the project, including the development and validation of lead MiTE molecules, thereby advancing the understanding of immune modulation in the tumor microenvironment and supporting the strategic goals of the research initiative.
